data.title: Billionaire Investor Warns Of 'Economic Nuclear Winter' -- Our Picks
data.description:
Billionaire investor Bill Ackman just sounded the alarm and stated that we are headed towards a "self-induced economic nuclear winter" unless President Trump reverses course on his tariff plans.

And before you discredit him as having "Trump Derangement Syndrome" or being a poor investor due to his few big losses, consider that he has actually been a strong Trump supporter, helping him win in the election, and despite having suffered some high-profile losses (such as Valeant), he still has one of the best track records in the world, having massively outperformed the rest of the market (SPY), resulting in him becoming a self-made billionaire.

I would add that he is not shorting the market, he has no margin or other liquidity issues, and most of his capital is permanent, so he is not worried about redemptions.

His only conflict, if it even is one, is that he is invested in the market and wants the American and global economies to do well in order to earn a good return on his investments.

So why is he sounding the alarm?

I have spent a lot of time reading his many tweets, responses, and retweets of other commentators.

My take is that he is very concerned about the broader economy because of the following 10 reasons:

  1. Tariffs could greatly increase the price of goods for consumers, reducing confidence and consumption.
  2. It could also increase the cost of goods for American businesses, reducing their profit margins and making them less competitive on the global stage.
  3. It will lead to retaliation from major trading partners, making it harder for American companies to sell their products abroad. China has already issued major tariffs on American companies, putting them at a major disadvantage in accessing its massive market.
  4. It will also lead to indirect economic pain from foreigners boycotting American products. Tesla (TSLA) is a prime example of that, as its foreign sales are down significantly, but other American products could also be impacted going forward.
  5. It will also lead to lower international tourism to the US. For example, flight bookings from Canada to the US are down 70%.
  6. It hurts the US Dollar by lowering the demand for it. It has gone down sharply in recent days, making it more expensive for Americans to import goods and travel abroad.
  7. It hurts business confidence because there is just too much uncertainty, causing companies to delay major investments and future hiring.
  8. The uncertainty also leads to a higher risk premium, increasing the cost of equity for companies. It makes it harder for public companies to raise capital, but even more so for smaller private companies.
  9. The US tariffs are now pushing foreign countries to create new economic relations that replace the US. As an example, China will seek to buy more soybeans from Brazil and tech from Japan and South Korea. In fact, China, Japan, and South Korea just held their first major economic dialogue in five years to coordinate a plan in response to the tariffs.
  10. Finally, it is not clear that it will revive domestic manufacturing. Even with major tariffs in place, it is still generally cheaper to produce in countries like Vietnam than in the US. Moreover, few companies will be ready to green-light major investments into building new manufacturing plants because there is just too much uncertainty. Trump has already shown with Canada and Mexico that he can impose major tariffs and talk tough, just to remove them a week later. Companies will not go on a 5-year journey to build a new plant if they think that tariffs could go away in a few months, or at the latest, when the next administration comes into power.

He has publicly called for President Trump to call a 90-day time-out to negotiate privately with other countries and protect the economy.

He says that otherwise, we will "destroy confidence in our country as a trading partner, as a place to do business, and as a market to invest capital."

In his most popular tweet, he adds that:

If, on the other hand, on April 9th, we launch an economic nuclear war on every country in the world, business investment will grind to a halt, consumers will close their wallets and pocketbooks, and we will severely damage our reputation with the rest of the world that will take years and potentially decades to rehabilitate.

What CEO and what board of directors will be comfortable making large, long-term, economic commitments in our country in the middle of an economic nuclear war? I don’t know of one who will do so.

When markets crash, new investment stops, consumers stop spending money, and businesses have no choice but to curtail investment and fire workers.

And it is not just the big companies that will suffer. Small- and medium-sized businesses and entrepreneurs will experience much greater pain. Almost no business can pass through an overnight massive increase in costs to their customers. And that’s true even if they have no debt, and, unfortunately, there is a massive amount of leverage in the system.

Business is a confidence game. The president is losing the confidence of business leaders around the globe. The consequences for our country and the millions of our citizens who have supported the president, in particular low-income consumers who are already under a huge amount of economic stress, are going to be severely negative. This is not what we voted for.

The President has an opportunity on Monday to call a time-out and have the time to execute on fixing an unfair tariff system.

Alternatively, we are heading for a self-induced, economic nuclear winter, and we should start hunkering down.

May cooler heads prevail. [emphasis added]

Ouch!

Some still hope that this is just a negotiation strategy to get other countries to remove trade barriers, but I am not so sure. He has been very consistent about this particular topic for decades, and he seems very serious if you listen to his recent discussions with the press.

The unfortunate reality is that the damage is already done.

Even if President Trump now pulls out and claims victory for winning some concessions, I expect still economic damage from this in the near term as this disruption will have led to significant uncertainty, hurt confidence, damaged international relationships, and will potentially lead to less investment going forward.

So, in my mind, the question is not if we are going into a recession. The question is how damaging it will be.

How should you invest in such a challenging environment?

Ideally, you would invest in market sectors that:

  • are recession-resistant,
  • not impacted by tariffs,
  • are not heavily leveraged,
  • can pass inflation on to clients,
  • and are priced at low valuations to offer margin of safety.

With that in mind, one of my favorite sectors of the market is that of real estate investment trusts, or REITs in short.

Most of them own highly resilient properties that our society cannot live without. Think of things like cell towers, affordable housing, farmland, hospitals, data centers, or grocery stores.

The demand for these properties is resilient to recessions, and they also enjoy long-term leases that result in highly consistent and predictable rental income even through recessions.

As a result, REITs (VNQ) have been far more stable than other stocks during most recessions:

They are also not heavily impacted by tariffs in most cases.

Major global companies like Nike (NKE) and Apple (AAPL) are heavily impacted, but REITs operate domestic businesses and do not trade with foreign countries.

Whether China imposes major tariffs or not, people will still use their phones to surf the web in the US, requiring cell towers. They will also need a roof over their heads, requiring apartments. They will need to buy, requiring grocery stores. Etc.

REITs are also in a strong financial position to withstand a crisis. Their leverage is at an all-time low, with debt-to-asset values at just 35%, and debt maturities are long and well-staggered.

Finally, and perhaps most importantly, REIT valuations are at the lowest level in a decade, with most REITs trading at low multiples of their cash flows and steep discounts relative to the private market value of their properties.

This should provide a margin of safety going forward, and it may explain why REITs have been outperforming the rest of the market so far this year:

Finally, the silver lining in all of this is that if we go into a recession, interest rates are likely to drop to much lower levels, and this should benefit REITs.

The 10-year Treasury has already dropped from nearly 5% earlier this year to just 4% today, which should allow REITs to refinance their debt at lower rates.

The tariffs will also increase the cost of construction, increase the replacement value of existing properties, reduce future supply, and accelerate rent growth in certain property sectors.

But what are the best REITs to buy today?

There are lots of opportunities right now, and it is difficult to pick just one.

There are many that we are accumulating at these levels at High-Yield Landlord.

One that we like a lot is called Alexandria Real Estate (ARE). It is the leader in the life science property sector. These healthcare properties are recession-resistant and should not be heavily affected by the trade wars because pharmaceuticals have been exempted from the tariffs.

Moreover, Alexandria has 8-year long leases on average to protect through the downcycle, and low leverage with a strong BBB+ investment grade rating.

Its valuation is at a decade low because its sector is today oversupplied, but rent should already accelerate in 2026 as new construction activity has dropped to historically low levels and will only drop further as a result of the tariffs.

To return to its historical average valuation multiple, the stock would need to more than double from here, and while we wait, we earn a near 7% dividend yield.

Current FFO Multiple Historic FFO Multiple
Alexandria Real Estate 8x FFO 20-25x

Another REIT that we like at these levels is BSR REIT (HOM.U:CA / OTCPK:BSRTF).

I think that we can all agree that affordable apartments that rent for $1,500 per month in rapidly growing Texan markets will remain in high demand, regardless of what happens to this trade war or the broader economy.

These properties have suffered stagnating rents in recent years due to oversupply, but the new supply has already dropped to a decade-low, and our prediction is that it will drop even more as construction costs will go way up as a result of the tariffs.

This should lead to a strong acceleration in rent growth over the coming years, and yet, the REIT is trading at a 35% discount today compared to our estimate of the fair market value of its properties. The upside potential is about 50%, and we earn a 4% monthly tax-advantaged dividend yield while we wait (classified as return of capital).

The yield may not seem that high, but this is only because the REIT retains nearly half of its cash flow to buy back shares.

Closing Note

The outlook is extremely uncertain right now, and we could face major pain before the economy recovers.

At High-Yield Landlord, we are playing defense by positioning our portfolio to own mostly essential infrastructure that's today discounted, offering high upside, steady cash flow, and inflation protection.

Manager Commentary

"Yields on longer-term U.S. government bonds moved higher in response to proposals around memorializing the original Trump tax cuts, increasing deductions for state and local taxes, as well as potential further miscellaneous tax reductions with no concrete plans to raise offsetting revenue.” - Jennison Associates, LLC

Market in Review

U.S. equities closed out a strong fourth quarter to end the year near record levels. The quarter’s gains came as voters went to the polls in early November and chose to return Donald Trump to the White House. Markets then moved higher, reflecting enthusiasm over campaign promises from the president-elect to relax regulatory oversight aimed initially at the Financial Services and Energy sectors. At the same time, investors faced the prospects of increasing tariffs and greater trade friction.

Yields on longer-term U.S. government bonds moved higher in response to proposals around memorializing the original Trump tax cuts, increasing deductions for state and local taxes, as well as potential further miscellaneous tax reductions with no concrete plans to raise offsetting revenue. The U.S. 10-year Treasury yield closed 2024 at ≈4.5% versus ≈3.9% at the start of the year. For the period, the U.S. dollar gained against other currencies on the presumption that the new president’s “America First” agenda will likely come at the expense of growth in other parts of the globe.

Moreover, the pace of U.S. economic activity continued its moderating trend. The Federal Reserve (“Fed”), in response, trimmed an additional 0.25% bringing its target federal funds rate to 4.25%–4.50%. The effective level of the funds rate closed the year at ≈4.3% versus ≈5.3% when the year began. Unemployment reached 4.2% at the end of November, up approximately 0.50% from the year-earlier level.

Portfolio Performance

The Harbor Long Term Growers ETF (“ETF”) returned 5.97% (at NAV) underperforming the benchmark, the Russell 1000® Growth Index, which returned 7.07% during the fourth quarter of 2024.

Among the benchmark’s largest sectors, Consumer Discretionary and Communication Services outperformed the broad index. Meanwhile, Information Technology also advanced but underperformed the benchmark. Health Care lost ground during the period.

Stock selection within the Consumer Discretionary and Health Care sectors detracted the most from relative performance during the period. Security selection within the Consumer Staples, Information Technology, and Communication Services sectors benefited relative results. Sector allocation effects are residual to our bottom-up process; however, they were a benefit to relative return for the quarter. Our overweights to Consumer Discretionary and Communication Services helped, while our overweight to Health Care detracted.

Contributors and Detractors

Shares of Eli Lilly (LLY) detracted from performance due to the company’s continued challenges in forecasting shorter-term revenue and profits for its weight-loss/obesity and diabetes franchises as well as supply issues. Shares of Novo Nordisk (NVO) lost value due to disappointing clinical trial results for CagriSema, the company’s latest weight-loss drug.

Shares in Amazon (AMZN) contributed to performance for the period due to optimism about the company’s cloud business and advancements in its AI-driven innovations. NVIDIA (NVDA) benefited from strong demand and a backlog of orders for its graphics processing, as well as excitement for its next-generation Blackwell chips.

Buys and Sells

We purchased Edwards Lifesciences (EW), the leader in the global transcatheter valve market. The company offers premium growth in the medical devices industry. In the markets Edwards competes in, it has the No. 1 or No. 2 share. The company’s structural heart/valves business continues to grow, and the firm offers innovation within this market. At the same time, non–transcatheter aortic valve replacement technologies are growing fast and becoming a greater contributor to the whole company. Meanwhile, the firm is focusing on expanding its footprint in Asia and Latin America, where cardiovascular diseases are prevalent, potentially capturing new market segments and driving revenue growth.

We eliminated MongoDB (MDB) from the portfolio due to the company’s decelerating revenue trajectory and contracting margins.

Overweights and Underweights

Sector weights are driven by our fundamental research-based stock selection. As of Dec. 31, 2024, the portfolio’s largest sector weights were Information Technology, Consumer Discretionary, and Communication Services. The largest sector overweights relative to the Russell 1000® Growth Index were in Communication Services and Consumer Discretionary. The largest underweight relative to the Russell 1000® Growth Index was in Information Technology (due to the large benchmark weights in Apple, Microsoft, and NVIDIA).

Outlook

We exited 2024 on the back of several strong years of share-price recovery, particularly for growth stocks, following the sharp declines in late 2021 through 2022. The strength of corporate profits overall has been an important contributor amid sustained growth of the U.S. economy. Employment has remained at healthy levels throughout the period, but with signs of weakening in the past six months. While the rate of inflation has continued to decline, trends have also flattened out in recent months. The path to lower short-term interest rates rests on the dynamics between these factors, and we have less clarity in direction at the end of 2024 than we had at the beginning of the year.

Against this backdrop, the portfolio continues to be anchored on profits growth and longer-term opportunity. All told, we are pleased by the operating results of most of our holdings in 2024. We are also encouraged by the tremendous progress that has been made in the foundational layer of generative AI since the launch of ChatGPT approximately two years ago. Many of the portfolio’s best performing names have benefited from these capital investments, though we are cognizant of the valuation levels reached over the period. Cutbacks of semiconductor holdings over the past year were undertaken with this in mind, while we remain optimistic on the sector in general and the emerging opportunities to monetize generative AI through software and across industries in the years ahead. As with the previous two years, we continue to see revenue and profit gains for portfolio holdings growing at faster rates than the averages against which we compare, while the portfolio enjoys a greater degree of diversification and less concentration.

Disclosures

Expense ratio information is as of the Fund's current prospectus, as supplemented. Gross expenses are the Fund's total annual operating expense.

Due to the security valuation procedures of the Fund and intra-day trading activity not included in the FactSet calculations, the actual returns may vary. From time to time, the cash return in the portfolio may appear distorted based on the way FactSet’s attribution calculation methodology addresses delayed settlements.

Best and Worst Performers sections reflect stocks in the portfolio for the quarter with an average weight of 0.25% or greater.

Views expressed herein are drawn from commentary provided to Harbor by the subadvisor and may not be reflective of their current opinions or future actions, are subject to change without prior notice, and should not be considered investment advice.

This information should not be considered as a recommendation to purchase or sell a particular security. The weightings, holdings, industries, sectors, countries, and returns mentioned may change at any time and may not represent current or future investments.

As a result of changing market conditions, total net asset levels, expenses and other statistics may change at any time and may differ from those shown.

The total amount shown for sector, industries, or country holdings may be greater than 100% because of the inclusion of derivatives and the collateral securities supporting those instruments. Sector allocations are determined using the Global Industry Classification Standard (GICS), which is a service of Morgan Stanley Capital International (MSCI) and Standard & Poor's (S&P).

Investors should carefully consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of a fund before investing. To obtain a summary prospectus or prospectus for this and other information, visit harborcapital.com or call 800-422-1050. Read it carefully before investing.

Foreside Fund Services, LLC is the Distributor of the Harbor ETFs.

Attribution Disclosures

Linked Performance by Sectors data is produced from FactSet using data supplied by State Street Bank and Trust Company. Active Currency Contribution is the Currency Contribution of the portfolio minus the Currency Contribution of the benchmark.

Allocation Effect is the portion of portfolio excess return that is attributable to taking different group bets from the benchmark. (If either the portfolio or the benchmark has no position in a given group, allocation effect is the lone effect.) A group's allocation effect equals the average percent capitalization of the portfolio's group minus the average percent cap of the benchmark's group times the total return of the benchmark group minus the total return of the benchmark.

Average Weight is the dollar value (price times the shares held) of the security or group, divided by the total dollar value of the entire portfolio displayed as a percentage. It is calculated as the simple arithmetic average of daily values.

Contribution to Return is the contribution of a security or group to the overall portfolio return. It is calculated as the security weight multiplied by the daily security return linked daily across the reporting period. Currency Contribution is Total Return in USD subtracting out the Local Returns.

Local Returns are the Total Return of the portfolio or benchmark using the local currency.

Selection Effect is the portion of portfolio excess return attributable to choosing different securities within groups from the benchmark. A group's security selection effect equals the average weight of the benchmark's group times the total return of the portfolio's group minus the total return of the benchmark's group.

Total Effect is the sum of Allocation Effect and Selection Effect. The total effect represents the opportunity cost of what was done in a group relative to the overall portfolio. It is not just the difference between percent contribution in the portfolio and benchmark. At the overall portfolio level, the two numbers are equal. At the group level, they can be different.

Total Return is the price change of a security or group including dividends accrued over the report period (or the in-portfolio return) which includes only the time period that each security was in the portfolio.

Definitions

Beta is a measure of systematic risk, or the sensitivity of a fund to movements in the benchmark. A beta of 1 implies that the expected movement of a fund's return would match that of the benchmark used to measure beta.

Median Market Cap: The median size of the companies in a portfolio or index as measured by the market value of outstanding shares.

Weighted Average Market Capitalization: The average size of the companies in a portfolio or index as measured by the market value of outstanding shares. Earnings per share is the portion of a company's profit allocated to each outstanding share.

The Est 3-5 Yr EPS Growth (%) is the estimated growth of earnings per share over the next 3-5 years, using pre-calculated mean long-term EPS growth rate estimates, which are calculated using each individual broker’s methodology, from FactSet, First Call, I/B/E/S Consensus, and Reuters. Forward looking estimates may not come to pass.

% EPS Growth - Past 3-Year: Earnings per share refers to the bottom-line measure of a company’s profitability defined as net income divided by the number of outstanding shares. The Adjusted Trailing P/E (price-to-earnings) Ratio is the closing stock price divided by the sum of the last 12 months actual EPS.

Forecasted P/E Ratio: a measure of the P/E (price-to-earnings) ratio using forecasted earnings for the P/E calculation. Return on Equity is a measure of financial performance calculated by dividing net income by shareholders' equity. The Price/Book (price-to-book) Ratio evaluates a firm's market value relative to its book value.

All P/E, ROE and P/B statistics are calculated as weighted medians.

Original Post

data.title: EZA: Great Dividends But Low Growth
data.description:
Introduction

As one of the wealthiest countries in terms of private wealth, South Africa is one of the African countries that offers the most investment opportunities. While the iShares MSCI South Africa ETF (NYSEARCA:EZA) gives access to this market and provides a high dividend yield, growth will likely continue being low but positive.

ETF Key Data

EZA tries to give investors access to the South African markets. At an expense ratio of 0.59%, the ETF is rather cheap compared to other iShares country ETFs that normally have an expense ratio of 0.95%. Furthermore, EZA has a dividend yield of 7.37%, making it extremely efficient in generating income. This is even amplified by an expected dividend growth rate of over 150%. When looking at a daily share volume of over 200k, the ETF is generally liquid.

When looking at the ETFs sector allocation (Figure 1), one can see that EZA is very concentrated among the 3 sectors of Financials, Basic Material, and Communication. The low diversification can also be seen in Figure 2, highlighting the ETFs' top 10 holdings. Here, Naspers Ltd is overweight at 16.8%, more than the second and third highest weighting combined. Also, the ETF only holds 36 assets, and the top 10 assets make up more than 60% of the whole portfolio. This can also be seen in Figure 3, showing the performance metrics of the ETF in comparison to the S&P 500. While EZA showed a return of 7.5% since 2003, its volatility and max. Drawdowns are much higher than that of American stocks. At a P/B ratio of 1.81 and a P/E of 12.95, the ETF is somewhere between cheap and normally priced.

Economic Conditions

Currently, South Africa only has a low GDP growth of 0.9% and has been around this level for the last 10 years. Meanwhile, unemployment is very high at 31.9%, even higher than pre-pandemic levels between 24% and 30.1%. Over the past few years, inflation has come down significantly. While it was between 4% and 5% for the majority of time over the last 10 years, it now is at 3.2%. Nevertheless, a small upward trend can be seen over the past few months, where the bottom was at 2.8% in October. This brings us to interest rates. Overall, the South African central bank is currently in a cutting cycle, which could produce an economic tailwind. Nevertheless, the increase in inflation makes it unlikely that interest rates will come down to pre-2022 levels of 4.25% from their current level of 7.5%. Additionally, the country runs a trade surplus while having a debt / GDP ratio of 72.2%.

Politics

South Africa is currently governed by the African National Congress (ANC). Economically, they aim to stabilize the country's economy by running a budget surplus and stabilizing the debt / GDP ratio at 76.2%. They introduced a VAT tax, and key funding is allocated to education, social protection, and healthcare. Furthermore, they plan to invest in infrastructure and improve electricity supply as well as water quality and digital communication. While the next election is at the end of May this year, the ANC is still polling at 43%. This will likely leave them as the governing party but will cost them their absolute majority, likely forcing them to work with the Democratic Alliance (DA), a party in favor of market-driven reforms and growth.

Overall, it seems like the current South African government is making better policies for bondholders than for shareholders. While infrastructure spending could boost growth, and education is very important for long-term growth, it is unlikely to enhance GDP growth to higher levels. This especially holds with the planned debt / GDP stabilization at 76.2%, which does not give the government much more room for further investment. While the election might change that, the impact of the DA will have to be seen and is likely not high. Furthermore, there is the risk of the ANC building a coalition with a Marxist party should they not find enough common ground with the DA.

Technicals

From a technical perspective, EZA does not look interesting at the moment. While 1-year momentum is slightly positive, 6-month momentum is at -17.93%. Additionally, the index is trading well below its 200-day simple moving average, suggesting a weak short-term outlook.

Peer Comparison

When comparing EZA to the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY), its valuation is, of course, a plus. Nevertheless, the US is overall accepted as a politically stable (at least until recently) location for investments. Furthermore, it is known for innovation, while South Africa seems to be stagnating and has generally higher GDP growth.

Meanwhile, in comparison to other, more fast-growing markets, such as Singapore, where I recently gave a "buy" rating, EZA looks unattractive. At a 6-month momentum of 1.37% and a 1-year momentum of almost 19%, iShares MSCI Singapore ETF (EWS) just looks better at the moment. In this case, even EZAs low valuation is no real help as EWS is almost equally expensive at a P/E ratio of 13.05 and a P/B ratio of 1.57 (for EZA it was 12.95 and 1.81 respectively). The only advantage for South Africa between the ETFs would be that EZA has the higher dividend yield. Figure 4 shows the correlation between EZA, EWS, and SPY. As can be seen, the EZA has a 70% correlation to both ETFs, allowing for some but not very big diversification.

Conclusion

At the current moment, there does not seem to be much change happening in South Africa. While the elections bring some chances and risks, they are unlikely to have a big effect, as the ANC will continue leading the country. While this is good for financial stability, it is unlikely to entice much more investment and economic growth. The same goes for interest rates, which might see some, but not many, cuts due to the already rising inflation. I give EZA a solid “hold” rating, as it is an interesting way to diversify and enhance one's dividend yield. Nevertheless, there is not enough growth potential to put a buy rating on it at this time.

data.title: Rare Stock Picks In March 2025 - From 15 Discerning Analysts
data.description:
Following the sell-off that began at the beginning of March (and accelerated in the first week of April), are there any compelling investment ideas out there that may have flown under your radar?

Below is a list of March Buy recommendations made by analysts who had no other bullish recommendations over the past 3 months.

For your directory assistance, we've classified the opportunities by sector.

Healthcare

EssilorLuxottica (OTCPK:ESLOF) - Investment analyst Harry Lynch says it is an industry leader with great economies of scale, great management and strong brand recognizability - the analyst plans to buy on any price weakness. - EssilorLuxottica: Quality Company, Here For The Long Haul

Financials

Sachem Capital (SACH) - Longtime analyst Miguel Marecos Duarte says the market's overly pessimistic view and management's improvements present a favorable investment opportunity while it has taken steps to stabilize its loan book, return to profitability, and reinstate its dividend. - The Market Is Too Pessimistic On Sachem Capital

KKR Real Estate Finance Trust (KREF) - Longtime analyst Allen Cooke says it is trading at a 25% discount to book value with a 9% yield, while improvements in commercial real estate are creating portfolio stabilization. - Improving Outlook At KKR Real Estate Finance Trust

D.R. Horton (DHI) - SCIRE Investments, who has an opportunistic investment approach across the capital structure, says its current valuation and intermediate-to-long term growth prospects present an attractive entry point for long-term investors, as it has a history of market share growth, strong management, and prudent capital allocation. - D.R. Horton: A Coffee Can Compounder

Industrials

OPENLANE (KAR) - Longtime analyst Mike Arnold says it trades with an attractive 9-10x EBITDA multiple (a significant discount to peers despite strong growth) giving investors a near-term and long-term shot on goal to make solid returns while it operates in a fragmented market that is ripe for consolidation. - Will Openlane Appoint A CFO Or Sell Itself, That Is The Question

data.title: The Manitowoc Company: Shares Have Gotten Dirt Cheap
data.description:
When it comes to investing, I have a pretty good track record. But nobody, and I do mean nobody, has a perfect record. Unfortunately, one company that I have been wrong about so far is The Manitowoc Company (MTW). This is a business that produces and sells equipment such as hydraulic cranes, lattice boom crawler cranes, boom trucks, tower cranes, and more. Back in February of last year, I believed that the enterprise was attractively priced. Shares were cheap and, while there were some weak spots, the overall trend for revenue leading up to that point had been encouraging. We were starting to see some meaningful declines, such as revenue later in that year and backlog both falling. But even factoring that weakness into the equation, the stock looked cheap enough to justify a ‘buy’ rating.

Since that time, the S&P 500 is down 0.4%. Over the same window of time, however, The Manitowoc Company has experienced a downside of 40.9%. This comes on the back of mixed financial results and concerns about the economy more broadly. While I do think that current conditions could pose a problem for the firm, the stock is truly too cheap to ignore. Because of broader economic concerns at the moment, I won't go so far as to upgrade the company from a ‘buy’ to a ‘strong buy’. But if we do see this tariff problem that we are contending with disappear in short order, then an upgrade would certainly be justified.

A look at recent results

Fundamentally speaking, the picture has been rather mixed for The Manitowoc Company as of late. Take the 2023 to 2024 fiscal years as an example. Year over year, revenue contracted from $2.23 billion to $2.18 billion. This drop was driven by weakness across two of the three geographic regions of the company. In the Americas, revenue declined from $1.21 billion to $1.20 billion. This drop of 1.1% was because of an unfavorable change in product mix.

In its EURAF (Europe and Africa) segment, the company saw an even more drastic decline of 8% from $669.6 million to $616 million. A reduction in new crane shipments was the big driver here. However, management did not really provide much in the wave detail as to why this occurred. The only strength for the company came from its MEAP (Middle East and Asia Pacific) segment. Revenue for that part of the company actually jumped by 5% from $347 million to $364.4 million. This was because of higher non-new machine sales caused by an increase in demand for crane shipments. The firm also saw an $8.1 million rise in revenue associated with new machine sales thanks to a favorable change in product mix.

With revenue falling, would expect profitability to drop as well. However, the situation was more complicated than that. Net income actually rose from $39.2 million to $55.8 million. However, this requires some explanation. Part of this improvement came from a reduction in engineering, selling, and administrative costs from $328.3 million to $315.7 million. This stemmed from a $12.3 million reduction in legal costs associated with an EPA situation. Lower employee related costs of $3.1 million also helped. An even bigger contributor, however, was the company received a $44.1 million tax benefit for the year. That compared to the $5 million provision that it had to set aside the previous year. Even on an adjusted basis, there was a decline, with profits falling from $54.5 million to $14.7 million.

Other profitability metrics were, quite frankly, depressing. Operating cash flow as an example. This fell from $63 million to $49.2 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a fall from $110.6 million to $49.4 million. Obviously, we should also be paying attention to EBITDA. In 2023, this metric was $175.3 million. Fast-forward one year later, and it had fallen to $128.4 million.

In the final quarter of 2024, The Manitowoc Company began to show some more favorable results. In the chart above, you can see that revenue, profits, and three of the four cash flow metrics for the company improved. While this could be just a blip on the radar, it's more likely the start of consistent improvement for the company.

For 2025, management expects revenue to be between $2.175 billion and $2.275 billion. At the midpoint, that would be 3.3% above what the company generated in 2024. They also expect EBITDA to be between $120 million and $145 million. Adjusted earnings per share of between $0.15 and $0.85 should translate to adjusted net profits, at the midpoint, of $17.8 million. And finally, it is looking as though operating cash flow will be between $102 million and $132 million.

Using these estimates, as well as historical figures for the 2024 fiscal year, you can see how the stock is currently valued. On an absolute basis, shares strike me as incredibly cheap. This is especially the case if we assume that 2025 figures end up coming into play. Even relative to similar companies, shares are a bit on the cheap side. In the chart below, I stacked The Manitowoc Company up against five other similar enterprises. On a price to operating cash flow basis, you can see that only two of the five companies are cheaper than it is. This number drops to one of the five if we use the EV to EBITDA approach.

Long term, I believe that this is a very attractive candidate. But short term, it could be a very bumpy ride. Because of the recently announced reciprocal tariffs that have been applied by the Trump Administration, and the prospect of retaliatory tariffs, there could be some near term weakness. About 48.7% of the company's revenue comes from the US. However, the rest comes from overseas, with 27.5% of total sales attributable to Europe. Now, to be fair, as the image below illustrates, Europe has become less important to the company over the past few years. This has been offset by growth in the US and growth in other countries. But that growth in other countries could also come under pressure because of tariffs, since the recently announced ones apply to the globe.

In addition to a majority of revenue coming from overseas, it is worth noting that most of what the company produces very likely comes from outside of the US. And even what is produced in the US almost certainly relies on heavily imported parts. The reason why I say very little likely comes from the US is because, of the material manufacturing facilities that the company has in operation, only one of them, out of eight, is located here at home. They have one in Germany, one in Italy, two in France, one in Portugal, one in China, and another in India.

Another issue is that backlog and orders have been quite weak. In the chart below, you can see precisely what I mean. By the end of the most recent quarter, backlog had fallen to $650.2 million. That's down from the $917.2 million reported one year earlier. The good news is that we have seen orders improve slightly, rising from $475.7 million to $515.6 million. But even with that increase on a year-over-year basis, orders are still too low to allow the backlog to grow, let alone stabilize. This tariff situation risks making that picture worse.

Takeaway

At this time, I believe that The Manitowoc Company is a really attractive prospect. I am worried about the situation involving tariffs. Only because of that have I decided not to upgrade the company. However, in the event that we do see some resolution on that front, this stock would be cheap enough to justify a very bullish outlook. For now, however, I am keeping it rated a ‘buy’.

The fourth quarter of 2024 delivered mixed investment results across risk assets, with investor focus squarely centered on the U.S. elections and resulting policy as the prevailing driver of asset returns. U.S. equities added to gains from previous quarters as the S&P 500 Index returned 2.4% during the fourth quarter, extending year-to-date gains to 25%. Fixed-income markets experienced elevated volatility during the quarter, driven by increased geopolitical tensions, fluctuating inflation expectations, and changes in central bank policies. Our business cycle regime indicator continues to signal late-cycle economic conditions with market sentiment exhibiting risk-seeking behavior. Central banks around the world continued to cut interest rates in response to increased confidence from authorities that we are well past peak inflationary pressures. data.title: Retiring On Passive Income
data.description:
Co-authored with Beyond Saving

Today, I want to talk about "rules". We at High Dividend Opportunities have developed a strategy that we call it, "the Income Method". We created various rules that we follow when managing our investment accounts. Let me be clear: These are purely voluntary guidelines that we place on ourselves to ensure that we are not taking unnecessary risks.

We often receive questions looking for a verdict on how our rules should be applied. For example, a common question is about our rule to limit position sizes to 3-4% for each holding. What if the same company has two different securities, like a bond and a common stock? Should we limit ourselves to 3% each for 6% combined? Or should we split our 3% investment between the two?

These aren't laws that are meant to be rigidly applied, and they aren't engraved on stone tablets. These are not based on some fundamental Truth. These are guidelines that we voluntarily follow because we believe they help make our portfolio stronger.

Sometimes, rules can be broken. Growing up, mom had a rule: No eating dinner in front of the TV. We ate as a family at the dinner table with no TV on. A good rule that led to healthy family conversation and bonding. But sometimes, mom would pick up a pizza and put a movie on the TV, and we would all sit on the floor, eating our dinner while watching it. Did that occasional rule-breaking harm anything? No.

If you understand why a rule exists, you can follow the spirit of the rule even if you occasionally violate the letter of the rule. Exercising a little bit of discretion and common sense is an important part of managing your portfolio.

We have the Rule of 42, the Rule of 25, we limit individual position sizes, and we have guidelines for our decision-making process.

Why We Have Rules

We can do anything we want. When we go to buy stocks, our broker isn't going to stop us from dumping 100% of our net worth and whatever amount of debt we can borrow into whatever meme stock the internet forums say we should HOLD to the moon with diamond hands.

We create rules for ourselves, and we do it because we aren't 20-somethings who feel invincible. We have learned, probably through making several big mistakes, that we are fallible. If you managed to be one of those smart people who learned solely through the mistakes of others, congratulations! Most of us learned that we aren't perfect through the School of Hard Knocks.

We all make mistakes, and when making investments with imperfect information about the future, we aren't going to be perfect. We are going to have some great investments, a lot of mediocre investments, a few bad investments, and a few really terrible investments—that's investing.

The problem is that you don't know what you are wrong about until the future proves to you that you were wrong. By that time, it's entirely too late.

Sometimes, we get things wrong at HDO. Our official track record is that we have exited 362 positions, and 80% of the time, we have exited with a profit. That means that 20% of the time, we realized a loss. We will always try to do our best, but the reality of the world is that we're never going to be perfect. We are always going to have some percentage of investments that result in a loss.

As we go into retirement, it is important that we recognize that we aren't going to be perfect. We are going to buy into investment ideas that don't work out, either through our own mistakes during due diligence or through a series of unfortunate events.

By creating guidelines, we can limit the impact our mistakes have on our overall portfolio, our income, and our retirement. It's ok to make mistakes, but it is not ok to have a retirement plan that allows room for mistakes.

The Rule Of 42

The "Rule of 42" is our target to diversify our portfolio among a minimum of 42 investments. Note that this is a minimum. The HDO Model Portfolio holds more than 120+ investments comprising common equities, funds, preferred equities, and bonds. I own them all, plus some additional holdings that don't quite fit into the Model Portfolio's specific goals.

In short, if you are following HDO, you should have no problem exceeding the target of 42 holdings, even if you find yourself disagreeing on the attractiveness of a particular investment or sector that we invest in. I believe that owning the entire HDO Model Portfolio is a good goal, and that's what I do myself.

Why is 42 the minimum? Well, because around 40 stocks, the benefit of diversifying further starts to fade. Source

It is easy to see how there is a huge diversification benefit for adding one stock to a portfolio that only has 1–2 stocks. But if your portfolio had 100 stocks, would adding one more make a material difference to your diversification? No.

The larger the portfolio, the less diversification benefits you get from adding one more investment. Around 40 stocks are where you have diversified almost all of the diversifiable risk.

We set the "Rule of 42" at 42, as a reminder not to take ourselves too seriously. 42 is the answer to "the Ultimate Question of Life, the Universe, and Everything". If you are at 38 tickers, you don't have to rush out and randomly buy four more. It's a target that you want to get to as you identify good investment opportunities. In time, it is a target you will likely exceed by a substantial amount.

We also want to apply some common sense to this target. If we buy 42 different oil investments, we aren't diversified. If we buy 42 REITs, we aren't diversified. As we add or remove holdings from the HDO Model portfolio, we are aware of whether we have similar holdings. We will frequently have 1-2 other stocks that are directly related because we do want to diversify within sectors, but we limit ourselves to having 2–3 holdings that are direct competitors. If you are assembling 42 holdings on your own, you'll want to take that step of asking how similar they are to each other.

Always keep the spirit of the rule in mind. The goal of Rule of 42 is to diversify our portfolios.

Allocation Limits

At HDO, we typically suggest limiting allocation sizes to 2% for single company tickers and 3-4% for funds. The reason for these limits is similar to the Rule of 42. If we have 42 holdings, our average position size will be 2.38%.

But sometimes, you might have a holding that outperformed recently, and now it is a larger allocation. Or maybe you just got a bit enthusiastic and bought more. If you have the common and the preferred for the same company, should you combine the allocations?

Let's go back to the core of why we have a limit.

Sometimes, we are going to be wrong. Sometimes, investments will cut their dividends, change their strategies, prices will crash, and sometimes companies go bankrupt.

When limiting our allocation, our goal is to limit the impact that any one bad investment will have on our retirement. We want to allow for catastrophe, whether it is something we should have predicted or something that completely blind sides us. Our allocations should be at a size where the following two things are true:

  1. If the investment is a complete disaster that results in a complete loss, the size is small enough that it will not materially impact our ability to continue funding our retirement.
  2. When bad news comes, the investment will be a small enough portion of our portfolio that panic will not have a significant influence on our decision-making.

The first one is easy. If we assume a 100% loss, which even in extreme cases is larger than what typically happens, losing 2-3% of your portfolio is a moderately bad week in the market. It isn't necessarily going to guarantee that you have a bad year. The stock market makes much larger gyrations all the time.

The second one is more individualized. Some investors can face huge losses in their portfolio and maintain the ability to keep a clear head and make rational decisions. Many other investors can't. I believe that with position sizes of 2-3%, most investors are able to look at bad news for a position without panic. They are comfortable with making the decision to hold or take the loss based on their outlook of what might happen in the future, rather than out of fear for their financial situation. If you find yourself getting overly stressed when a particular investment swings 10-20%, then perhaps your position sizes should be smaller. Only you can say where the line is that is comfortable for you.

When it comes to a company where you might hold bonds, preferred, and/or common equity, I suggest going through the philosophical exercise of the "FBI Gut check". Pretend you wake up tomorrow morning and the FBI is raiding the offices of the company, and it is going to be the next Enron, which means a big fat zero from the entire capital stack. Fortunately, those events are very rare, but it does happen. Fraud does occur. So, whenever you are asking, "is it ok to own 5%, 6%, or 10% in this one company?" Do the FBI gut check and think of how you would feel.

If you are concerned about the security of your retirement, then your allocation is too high. If you could brush it off as bad luck and the bruising is mostly your ego, but you wouldn't be concerned about your ability to sustain your retirement, then your allocation is fine.

That line isn't the same for everyone.

The Rule of 25

The "Rule of 25" is the idea that you should reinvest 25% of your dividend income. We implement this rule for a variety of reasons.

First, we understand that we aren't perfect, and we will sometimes invest in companies that cut their dividends. By reinvesting at least 25% of our income, we ensure that we have a cushion so that dividend cuts can be absorbed without putting our daily budget at risk. A 25% cushion is likely larger than you will ever need, but it is better to be cautious. You don't want to be in a situation where your portfolio isn't producing enough to meet your basic essentials. By planning to reinvest 25%, you will have excess income to absorb cuts even through a period like the GFC or COVID, where dividend cuts were more frequent than usual.

Second, reinvesting dividends provides us with a reliable method of driving income growth. While many companies might raise their dividends, there is no guarantee that they will. When we take a portion of our dividends and reinvest them, our income grows even if the company never hikes its dividend. While we love it when the companies we invest in raise their dividends, having a built-in source of income growth through reinvestment is a powerful base that helps us with our goal of growing our income every single year—even in years when the economy is poor.

The more you reinvest, the faster your income will grow. So, investors should weigh their needs for cash today, with their desire to see greater growth in the future.

Conclusion

We think that the rules that we have in our Income Method are a good set of guidelines to help you ensure that your portfolio is protected, and that you have a retirement plan that can survive contact with reality. Our Income Method is an investment strategy that doesn't rely on us being perfect. It allows for mistakes, and the guidelines help us ensure that those mistakes won't threaten our overall retirement plan. If we happen to be perfect. . . Well, that isn't going to happen.

Do you have to strictly follow our guidelines to be successful? No. Maybe you own 35 stocks, or maybe you want to set 100 as your minimum. If you only need 50% of the cash flow from your portfolio to meet your retirement goals, maybe you are comfortable bumping up your allocation sizes and making bigger bets because it isn't a problem if one doesn't work out.

We present our strategy with rules that make sense for our average member. One size doesn't fit all, so feel free to customize your rules to fit your particular needs. What is most important is to understand why the rules are in place and the goals of those rules. It is important to construct your retirement plan with the understanding that you aren't perfect, you will make bad investments, and sometimes, the investment you thought was a "can't lose" idea loses.

The Income Method is a strategy that builds in the assumption that we will make mistakes. It helps us ensure that our mistakes don't put our entire retirement at risk.

data.title: Meta: Time To Buy The Fear [Upgrade]
data.description:
Investment thesis

My previous cautious thesis about Meta Platforms (NASDAQ:META) aged well, as the stock has dipped by 11% since early October. The stock is currently extremely attractively valued, especially considering its stellar revenue growth and operating leverage. The pace of innovation and commitment to become the backbone of the AI era are impressive. All in all, I upgrade META back to "Strong Buy".

Recent developments

The company released its latest quarterly earnings on January 29, which were accompanied by a confident dual beat against consensus estimates. Meta delivered a strong 20.6% YoY revenue growth in Q4, which was an acceleration compared to the previous quarter's revenue growth.

The adjusted EPS growth was even more impressive, as the metric increased from $5.33 to $8.02 on a YoY basis. It was high-quality EPS growth as it was fueled by the operating margin expansion from 43.6% to 45.3%. Meanwhile, the outstanding share count was stable over the last several quarters.

With a robust operating leverage, Meta's free cash flow generating capacity remains strong. As a result, the company boasts a fortress balance sheet with a massive $28 billion net cash position. Such a strong position means that Meta is not only ready to weather any storm but also exercises vast financial flexibility to invest in innovation and business expansion.

The company remains quite aggressive in its AI endeavors as it continues ramping up its data center investments. Meta plans to build another large data center in Wisconsin, worth $1 billion. This is not just a separate data center, but a part of the company's ambitious plans in driving the global AI revolution. Overall, to ensure that it possesses sufficient capacity to handle increasing data demands and complex computations, Meta plans to pour $65 billion in its AI infrastructure during 2025.

The company also works hard on making its Llama LLM offering among the most advanced. Meta recently started releasing its updated Llama 4 family of models, which is a very important milestone in building the company's moat in the AI era. New features include significant improvements in natural language processing, which enable more sophisticated and context-aware interactions across Meta's products. In addition, Llama celebrated 1 billion downloads in March 2025.

According to the official release from Meta, the Llama 4 family looks very good compared to major LLM offerings. For example, Llama 4 Scout outperforms models like Gemma 3, Gemini 2.0 Flash-Lite, and Mistral 3.1 across several parameters. The biggest advantage, in my opinion, is that it offers an industry-leading 10 million token context windows. In addition, there is Llama 4 Maverick, which surpasses GPT-4o and Gemini 2.0 Flash on various benchmarks including coding, reasoning, multilingual, long-context, and image benchmarks. The company also says that Llama 4 Maverick's performance is comparable to DeepSeek v3.1 (DEEPSEEK).

Among recent promising developments, there is also a collaboration with Ultimate Fighting Championship [UFC]. The partnership aims to integrate Meta AI, Meta Glasses, and Meta Quest, into UFC's content delivery. I think that it is a quite promising endeavor since UFC has been quite rapidly gaining popularity over the last few years, especially with Generation Z. Therefore, this step increases Meta's chances to successfully expand into new user demographics.

From the secular trends' perspective, the picture remains bright for Meta. The global digital advertising market continues demonstrating robust growth, which is expected to sustain for longer. According to the forecast from Grand View Research, the global digital advertising market is expected to deliver a 15.4% market CAGR over the next five years.

Stock seasonality trends also look quite positive as the May-August period historically has been extremely successful with 70%-80% win rates for each month. In addition, there were several bullish rating reiterations from Jefferies, Cantor Fitzgerald, Citizens Capital Markets, and KeyBanc over the last couple of weeks.

Valuation update

Despite having a low "F" valuation grade from Seeking Alpha Quant, I think that Meta's valuation ratios look great. This is because most of its current multiples are lower than historical averages. I am not comparing to the sector median because Meta's multi-billion social media audience together with stellar profitability make it an absolutely unique company.

Looking only at valuation ratios might be too risky because multiples are mostly focused on either past performance or only short-term future. Therefore, it is better to also simulate a discounted cash flow [DCF] model. The discount rate for my DCF is 9.36%, please see calculations below.

As a conservative investor, I prefer to use the FCF margin that excludes the stock-based compensation [SBC]. In Meta's case, its current ex-SBC FCF margin is 11.8%. This was used as my base-year assumption. Due to Meta's historically strong operating leverage, I incorporated a quite aggressive 200-bps yearly expansion of the metric.

Revenue assumptions are based on consensus projections as their trajectory looks sound and conservative with a steady growth rate deceleration. My base-case scenario's perpetual growth rate is also quite conservative at 3%. The total fair value estimate in this case is $1.63 trillion, indicating a 27% upside potential.

I think that assuming a 4% perpetual growth rate for a company like Meta is also reasonable, which was my second DCF scenario for META. With a more aggressive perpetual growth rate, the business's fair value is closer to $1.9 trillion.

Risks update

President Trump's tariffs shocked the entire stock market, as the popular Fear & Greed Index is currently close to zero. META has lost around 12% over the last five days, and it is very uncertain whether we will see a sharp rebound, or the panic will take over the stock market for longer. My approach is simple, I dollar-average high-quality stocks when valuations become more attractive and willing to hold them for years. Trying to capitalize on this short-term extreme volatility might be too risky.

The regulatory environment around digital advertising and social media is very dynamic across the world. For example, the UK's Online Safety Act mandates stringent content moderation, and non-compliance might result in substantial fines and penalties.

Mark Zuckerberg is certainly the major star in the company's leadership team, meaning that we do not know very much about other key executives. Therefore, the expected departure of the company's head of AI research adds some uncertainty around Meta's future plans and innovation pace in AI.

Bottom line

To conclude, META is currently very attractively valued and certainly deserves the rating to be upgraded back to "Strong Buy". The company's fundamentals keep improving, and there is great potential to become the backbone of the AI era.

data.title: Taking A Small Position In Stanley Black & Decker (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
It’s been about 3 ½ months since I announced to an indifferent world that I’m continuing to avoid Stanley Black & Decker Inc. (SWK) in an article with the stupendously creative title “Continue to Avoid Stanley Black & Decker.” Since then, the shares have returned a loss of about 31.25% against a loss of about 15.5% for the S&P 500. This is just the sort of result that my very fragile ego needs, and I’m going to milk every opportunity to revel in self-congratulation. I’m sure you can’t wait to read how in love with my own analysis I am. Unfortunately, though, there’s more to this article than self-love. When I recommended avoiding the stock, it was trading at $80.50. Today, it’s changing hands for $57. For that reason, it’s a much less risky investment today than it was when I last wrote about it a few months back. I therefore need to determine whether or not it represents good value. I’ll make that determination by looking at the latest financial results, and by comparing the stock to the risk-free Treasury Note.

It’s time again for my thesis statement. This is where I offer readers the gist of my thinking so they can get in, understand my thoughts, and get out before they’re exposed to too much of my self-congratulation and proper spelling. You’re welcome. Anyway, the last time I looked at this stock, the dividend yield was significantly below the yield on the Treasury Note. Today, though, the relationship has changed, and the dividend is now significantly higher than the yield on the 10-Year Note. While the problems I outlined about dividend sustainability that I wrote about previously persist, I think the probability of a cut is very low. I’m going to take advantage of the current market weakness by buying a small position in this business. We’re frequently told to “buy low”, and I feel the need to remind readers that in order to do that, you need to “pull the trigger” when it’s scary to do so.

Financial Snapshot

If you had only 2 years of history with this company, you’d be forgiven for being impressed by Stanley’s financial performance. Although sales were down about $415 million, or 2.6%, in 2024 compared to 2023, net income actually swung from a loss to a positive $286 million. Most of this improvement relates to a $997 million, or 8.4%, reduction in cost of sales. So, management managed to handle the drop in business quite well, and returned the company to profitability.

The problem emerges when you compare the latest financial results to previous years. For example, revenue in 2024 was about the same as it was in 2021, but net earnings are about $1.4 billion lower. That’s a “spicy meatball”, as the young people say.

Still, profitability is profitability, and if I determine that the dividend is sustainable, and if the stock represents good value, I’ll buy.

Dividend Sustainability

In my view, the sustainability of the dividend is of critical importance here. The reason is simple enough. If the dividend isn’t sustainable, it’s likely that the price will drop precipitously, as dividend investors become uncertain.

Although I love accrual accounting as much as it’s possible to love accrual accounting, I am of the view that when it comes to dividends and their sustainability, cash is king. So, I’m going to review Stanley’s likely sources, and uses of cash. I’m in the mood to start by looking at sources, so I’m going to start looking at sources of cash. As of the latest 10-K, the company has about $290.5 million in cash and equivalents on the balance sheet. Additionally, if we strip out the sales of businesses and sales of assets over the past four years, they’ve generated an average of $366.3 million in cash from operations. That written, over the same time period, they’ve generated about $4.877 billion from the sale of businesses, and about $71.6 million from the sale of other assets.

At the same time, the company has invested an average of about $455 million in the business annually.

In terms of obligations, I’ve plucked the following from page 42 of the latest 10-K for your enjoyment and edification.

It seems that the company is “on the hook”, as the cool kids say, for $1.855 billion this year, and an average of $959 million for each of the next two years.

Taking all of the above into account leads me to what I consider to be an awkward conclusion. If the company can continue to generate massive levels of cash from selling businesses, then the dividend is quite safe. If, as is more likely, however, they inevitably run out of businesses to sell, the dividend isn’t well covered in my view. Generating an average of $366 million, while needing to invest $455 million doesn’t fill me with optimism.

That written, I don’t think the company will do all that it can to cut or not continue to increase the dividend. This is, after all, a “dividend aristocrat”, and if we use the behaviour of real world human aristocrats as a guide, they’ll be willing to take on ever greater levels of debt to keep up appearances. So, I think cultural factors, more than economic ones, will continue to protect this dividend.

My regular readers know that when I wrote “good value” above, I meant “good value relative to the risk-free alternative.” In order for me to be convinced to buy these shares, I’d need them to offer a superior return when compared to the 10-Year Treasury Note. That’s what the next section of this article will focus on.

As usual, when I compare a stock to the Treasury Note, I review the rate at which the stock dividend needs to grow in order for the cash flows from each investment to be equal. I then work out whether or not this growth is likely given its financial history.

As of this writing, the yield on the 10-Year Treasury Note is about 4.4%, and the dividend yield is a very healthy 5.94%.

To refresh your understanding if your math’s a little weak, 5.94% is actually higher than 4.4%, so it’s possible for this dividend to actually drop and for the investors to still earn the same return as Treasury Note holders. If you were wondering at what rate the dividend can be cut for the cash flows to be equal, you’re in luck, because I’ve worked it out and have included my findings below. I hope this satisfies your boundless curiosity. Because the premium is so high, the dividend can fall at a CAGR of about 6.8% for the next decade for the cash flows to be equal. That’s a fairly decent margin of safety in my view.

How much extra income will a stock investor earn over the Treasury Note if the dividend grows at the same rate over the next decade as it has over this past one? For information’s sake, it’s grown at a CAGR of about 4% over the past decade, and I present the answer to that burning question in the table below. It seems that the stock investor will pick up an extra 62% over the Treasury Note holder. That makes Stanley a relatively rare stock in that it offers a premium to the risk-free alternative, and not a discount to it.

Since Stanley currently offers a decent risk premium, I’m willing to take a small position in this company. The shares may drop further from current levels, and if that happens I’ll buy more. The valuation seems reasonable, the company is profitable enough, and I highly doubt the dividend will be cut anytime soon. It would have to be cut materially to erode the risk premium, so I feel fairly confident in my bullish position here.

I’m going to finish this article with a small sermon, and I’m sure you can’t wait to be lectured at by some stranger on the internet. For months now I’ve been comparing every stock, usually unfavourably, to the Treasury Note. Many readers have been puzzled by this, and have objected that it’s not “fair” to compare the stock to a government bond. It would have been better, apparently, to do peer comparisons. In my view, that might lead me to discover which stock in a given industry is the “cleanest dirty shirt”, but would do nothing to help with risk-adjusted returns. I compare this and every other stock to the risk-free rate in order to benchmark it. If I cannot earn more from a risky stock than from the risk-free Treasury Note, why would I buy the stock? I’m earning less from it, while taking on more risk?! That makes zero sense, and this framework is what informs my analyses since the yield on the Treasury Note spiked.
